## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## Character
Spec body content rewrite — non-Windows platform depth. BUS0 architecture and skeleton stubs already in place from pace 1; this pace fills the sub-spec bodies and aligns garrison preconditions.

## Goal
Flesh body content of BUSJCM/L, BUSJIM/L, BUSJHM/L; align garrison-bash precondition list to reference invigilate-{macos,linux}.

## Boundary
- BUSJCM body: Remote Login enable (`systemsetup -setremotelogin on`), ssh-copy-id orchestration, `pmset` power config, Tailscale install/service. Preconditions, steps, guarantee, completion.
- BUSJCL body: ssh-copy-id orchestration, systemctl mask sleep targets (laptop/desktop hosts), tailscaled enable/start. Preconditions, steps, guarantee, completion.
- BUSJIM/L bodies: posture fact lists with read commands and expected values; failure messages point at handbook (operator-managed) or caparison (caparison-managed).
- BUSJHM/L bodies: operator-manual scope per platform — Tailscale install + login, anything genuinely operator-side.
- BUSJGB: precondition list references invigilate-{macos,linux}.
- BUS0 already carries the catalog entries and verb definitions for these platforms; do not re-edit BUS0 here.
- No bash code edits.

## Done when
- All non-Windows platform sub-spec bodies render coherently.
- BUSJGB preconditions align with invigilate-{macos,linux} references.
- bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green.

50	  caparison-managed posture). Single source of truth for "correctly-configured"
51	  per platform. Used twice: caparison's post-completion check, garrison's
52	  precondition check.

61	  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
62	- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /

106	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:

124	   expected phase boundary).
125	
126	Phase 2 (key auth only):
127	
128	8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
129	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
130	   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
131	   a session.
132	9. Verify post-restart admin session authenticates by key alone.
133	
134	Phase 3 (post-trust admin posture):
135	
136	10. Stage `rbtww-main` WSL distribution under admin user via `wsl --import`
137	    from Ubuntu-24.04 seed. Always purge-and-reimport; no skip-if-present.
138	11. Apply powercfg sleep/hibernate disable (idempotent).
139	12. Set Tailscale service StartupType Automatic; start service
140	    (idempotent).
141	13. Run invigilate-windows post-completion check; non-zero indicates
142	    caparison's operations did not take or operator-managed posture is
143	    wrong.

146	
147	Compact: enable Remote Login (`systemsetup -setremotelogin on`), orchestrate
148	ssh-copy-id, apply `pmset` power config, ensure Tailscale service
149	auto-start. Closes with invigilate-macos post-check.

152	
153	Compact: orchestrate ssh-copy-id, mask sleep/suspend/hibernate targets via
154	systemctl (laptop/desktop hosts only), enable tailscaled. Closes with
155	invigilate-linux post-check.

158	
159	Each invigilate-{platform} reads its posture fact list and asserts each
160	fact matches expected. Failure surfaces a diagnostic naming the wrong fact
161	and pointing to either the operator handbook (for operator-managed
162	posture) or caparison (for caparison-managed posture). No mutations, no
163	remediation. Invocation contexts: caparison's post-completion check,
164	garrison's precondition check.

167	
168	1. Invigilate-{platform} precondition check; failure surfaces a missing or
169	   stale admin posture before any destructive workload work.
170	2. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
171	   only (no password fallback).
172	3. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or
173	   platform equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files
174	   outside home are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
175	4. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
176	   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
177	5. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
178	   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
179	6. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded
180	   in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can

187	- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
188	- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden

211	- Garrison-Cygwin exercise

215	
216	- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
217	  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
218	  only.
219	- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
220	  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
221	- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
222	- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
223	  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
224	- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
225	  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
226	- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
227	- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
228	- **Cygwin / WSL inner sshd hardening** — caparison covers Windows OpenSSH
229	  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
230	  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
231	  caparison phase or sibling verb.
232	- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
233	  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
234	  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
235	  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
236	  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
237	  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
238	  a time, never both running concurrently).
239	- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
240	  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.
241	
242	## Standing Notes
243	
244	- **Caparison-Windows handles first-time admin trust establishment plus the
245	  broader admin posture (WSL distribution staging, sleep/hibernate disable,
246	  Tailscale service auto-start).** SSH-trust phases retain the prior
247	  fenestrate two-phase structure (password fallback on /dev/tty for first
248	  run; key auth thereafter; sshd restart kills the phase-1 session).
249	  Operator-manual Windows scope: install OpenSSH server, enable the
250	  service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes` temporarily, allow the firewall
251	  port, ensure the admin user has a known password, apply
252	  security-sensitive registry edits per BUSJHW
253	  (DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion etc.), install Tailscale + Run-Unattended
254	  + first auth, configure netplwiz auto-login.
255	- **Linux/Mac admin trust enters caparison via ssh-copy-id orchestration.**
256	  Caparison-{linux,macos} wraps the operator-manual ssh-copy-id flow plus
257	  per-platform host posture (sleep masking, Tailscale service). The
258	  operator still does the actual ssh-copy-id one-time setup (or equivalent
259	  pubkey-placement); caparison verifies and applies the rest.
260	- **Two-phase SSH-trust within caparison-windows is structural, not
261	  optional.** `Restart-Service sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any
262	  post-restart verification must happen over a fresh session. Bash
263	  orchestrator treats the post-restart disconnect as the expected phase
264	  boundary, not a failure. The post-trust admin posture phases (WSL stage,
265	  powercfg, Tailscale service) run after the phase-2 reconnect.
266	- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
267	  prompts interactively; caparison and garrison would hang. Operator
268	  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
269	  privkey-at-rest protection.
270	- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.**
271	  Operator pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal
272	  SSH key) before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a
273	  precondition.
274	- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
275	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Caparison-Windows stages it; garrison-WSL
276	  exports it as a seed for the workload's HKCU\\Lxss. Invigilate-windows
277	  asserts its presence as a posture fact.
278	- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
279	  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
280	  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
281	  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
282	  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
283	  daemon ever runs concurrently.
284	- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
285	  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion
286	  and path construction.
287	- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
288	  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
289	- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
290	  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
291	- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite,
292	  GitHub, rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
- `buw-jpS` — Privileged SSH (admin pass-through, all platforms) — 1
- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1

3. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or
   platform equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files
   outside home are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
4. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
5. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
6. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded
   in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can
   authenticate outbound to GitHub).
7. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.

## Heat Sequence

**Spec foundation:**
- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb (since broadened to
  caparison)

**Implementation:**
- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, fenestrate + garrison ceremonies, cleanup
  superseded colophons + handbook entries

**Caparison/Invigilate restructuring:**
- BUS0 vocabulary + skeleton stubs (BUSJPF→BUSJCW; new BUSJC[ML],
  BUSJI[WML], BUSJH[WML])
- Per-platform caparison and invigilate spec content (Windows depth first,
  then macOS/Linux)
- Bash alignment via comment annotations (no symbol renames; preserves
  grep-archaeology for the upcoming spec/implementation review cycles)

**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis

**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
- Garrison-WSL exercise
- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
  only.
- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
- **Cygwin / WSL inner sshd hardening** — caparison covers Windows OpenSSH
  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
  caparison phase or sibling verb.
- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
  a time, never both running concurrently).
- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.

## Standing Notes

- **Caparison-Windows handles first-time admin trust establishment plus the
  broader admin posture (WSL distribution staging, sleep/hibernate disable,
  Tailscale service auto-start).** SSH-trust phases retain the prior
  fenestrate two-phase structure (password fallback on /dev/tty for first
  run; key auth thereafter; sshd restart kills the phase-1 session).
  Operator-manual Windows scope: install OpenSSH server, enable the
  service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes` temporarily, allow the firewall
  port, ensure the admin user has a known password, apply
  security-sensitive registry edits per BUSJHW
  (DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion etc.), install Tailscale + Run-Unattended
  + first auth, configure netplwiz auto-login.
- **Linux/Mac admin trust enters caparison via ssh-copy-id orchestration.**
  Caparison-{linux,macos} wraps the operator-manual ssh-copy-id flow plus
  per-platform host posture (sleep masking, Tailscale service). The
  operator still does the actual ssh-copy-id one-time setup (or equivalent
  pubkey-placement); caparison verifies and applies the rest.
- **Two-phase SSH-trust within caparison-windows is structural, not
  optional.** `Restart-Service sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any
  post-restart verification must happen over a fresh session. Bash
  orchestrator treats the post-restart disconnect as the expected phase
  boundary, not a failure. The post-trust admin posture phases (WSL stage,
  powercfg, Tailscale service) run after the phase-2 reconnect.
- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
  prompts interactively; caparison and garrison would hang. Operator
  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
  privkey-at-rest protection.
- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.**
  Operator pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal
  SSH key) before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a
  precondition.
- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Caparison-Windows stages it; garrison-WSL
  exports it as a seed for the workload's HKCU\\Lxss. Invigilate-windows
  asserts its presence as a posture fact.
- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
  daemon ever runs concurrently.
- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion
  and path construction.
- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite,
  GitHub, rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

## Mount ₢A-AA6 — `bus-non-windows-platform-depth`

**Goal:** Fill body content for the six non-Windows BUSJ sub-spec stubs (Caparison/Invigilate/Handbook × macOS/Linux), and align BUSJGB's precondition list to reference invigilate-{macos,linux} — mirroring what landed for the Windows side at ₢A-AA7.

**Heat context** (`₣A- rbk-mvp-3-draft-windows-procedures`):
- Three verbs (Caparison/Invigilate/Garrison), per-platform sub-specs. Windows depth landed at commit `43074f79` (₢A-AA7 wrapped). This pace is the macOS/Linux mirror.
- Stubs confirmed in place: BUSJCM/L, BUSJIM/L, BUSJHM/L all 400–700 bytes; BUSJCW/IW/HW are 5–10K filled. BUSJGB is 2.9K and currently references invigilate-windows shape.
- Paddock's caparison-{macos,linux} sketches:
  - **macOS:** `systemsetup -setremotelogin on`, ssh-copy-id orchestration, `pmset` power config, Tailscale service auto-start; closes with invigilate-macos.
  - **Linux:** ssh-copy-id orchestration, `systemctl mask` sleep targets (laptop/desktop only), `tailscaled` enable; closes with invigilate-linux.

**Full docket:**

> **Character:** Spec body content rewrite — non-Windows platform depth. BUS0 architecture and skeleton stubs already in place from pace 1; this pace fills the sub-spec bodies and aligns garrison preconditions.
>
> **Goal:** Flesh body content of BUSJCM/L, BUSJIM/L, BUSJHM/L; align garrison-bash precondition list to reference invigilate-{macos,linux}.
>
> **Boundary:**
> - BUSJCM body: Remote Login enable, ssh-copy-id orchestration, `pmset`, Tailscale install/service. Preconditions/steps/guarantee/completion.
> - BUSJCL body: ssh-copy-id orchestration, systemctl mask sleep targets, tailscaled enable/start. Preconditions/steps/guarantee/completion.
> - BUSJIM/L bodies: posture fact lists with read commands + expected values; failure pointers to handbook (operator-managed) or caparison (caparison-managed).
> - BUSJHM/L bodies: operator-manual scope per platform — Tailscale install + login + anything genuinely operator-side.
> - BUSJGB: precondition list references invigilate-{macos,linux}.
> - BUS0 already carries the catalog entries and verb definitions for these platforms; do not re-edit BUS0 here.
> - No bash code edits.
>
> **Done when:** All non-Windows platform sub-spec bodies render coherently; BUSJGB preconditions align with invigilate-{macos,linux} references; `bash -n` green; `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` and `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` green.

**Name assessment:** Current silks `bus-non-windows-platform-depth` matches the docket — no rename needed.

**Proposed approach:**
- Read BUSJCW/IW/HW (Windows trio) to internalize the structural pattern landed at AA7 — preconditions block, numbered ceremony steps, guarantee, completion-check section, NOTE discipline.
- Fill BUSJCM, BUSJCL caparison ceremonies as compact bodies (paddock sketch is the skeleton); each ends with invigilate-{platform} post-check.
- Fill BUSJIM, BUSJIL posture fact lists; each fact = read command + expected value + failure pointer.
- Fill BUSJHM, BUSJHL operator handbooks scoped to genuinely operator-side work (Tailscale interactive auth, etc.).
- Edit BUSJGB precondition prose to point at invigilate-{macos,linux} instead of whatever the current shape is.
- Run `bash -n` smoke across affected scripts (no script edits, but cheap), then `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh`, then `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh`.

**Execution strategy:** Sequential, sonnet-tier. Six small spec bodies in the same family; the operation is mechanical pattern-mirroring from BUSJC/I/HW, not architecture. Files are independent edits but understanding the Windows-side pattern first is load-bearing for coherence — sequential read-then-write is faster than parallel guessing.

Ready to proceed?

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_handbook_windows}
3	
4	NOTE: Per-platform handbook surface for Windows — operator-manual scope
5	that {busn_caparison} cannot or should not automate. Renders to the
6	operator's terminal as the entry point into Windows-specific bootstrap
7	procedures: OpenSSH server install + reachability, security-sensitive
8	registry edits (UAC-required, e.g.,
9	`DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion`), Tailscale install + Run-Unattended
10	toggle + first auth, netplwiz auto-login, and any other Windows
11	posture an operator must establish before {bust_caparison_windows} can
12	run. No remote contact and no profile mutation. Steps missing from
13	this procedure will cause {bust_caparison_windows} to fail at the
14	point that depends on them.
15	
16	//axhos_step
17	. *Install OpenSSH Server*: enable the OpenSSH Server optional feature.
18	  GUI path: Settings → Apps → Optional features → Add a feature →
19	  search "OpenSSH Server" → Install.
20	  Elevated PowerShell equivalent:
21	  `Add-WindowsCapability -Online -Name OpenSSH.Server~~~~0.0.1.0`
22	  Verify installation with `Get-WindowsCapability -Online -Name OpenSSH.Server~~~~0.0.1.0`.
23	
24	//axhos_step
25	. *Set sshd to Automatic and start*: from an elevated PowerShell prompt,
26	  run `Set-Service -Name sshd -StartupType Automatic` then
27	  `Start-Service -Name sshd`.
28	  Verify the service is running with `Get-Service sshd` and confirm
29	  `Status` is `Running`.
30	
31	//axhos_step
32	. *Open the SSH port through Windows Firewall*: from an elevated
33	  PowerShell prompt, run:
34	  `New-NetFirewallRule -Name sshd -DisplayName 'OpenSSH Server (sshd)' -Enabled True -Direction Inbound -Protocol TCP -Action Allow -LocalPort 22`
35	  (substitute the non-default port if the BURN profile carries one).
36	  Recent Windows builds create this rule automatically during the
37	  OpenSSH install; this step is a verify-and-add fallback. Confirm
38	  the rule exists with `Get-NetFirewallRule -Name sshd`.
39	
40	//axhos_step
41	. *Enable temporary password authentication*: open
42	  `C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config` in an editor running as
43	  Administrator. Set `PasswordAuthentication yes`, save, then restart
44	  the service with `Restart-Service sshd`. This setting is
45	  intentionally temporary — {bust_caparison_windows} flips it back to
46	  `no` during phase 1. Caparison's first-run admin SSH session uses
47	  `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` and falls through to a
48	  TTY password prompt; this step makes that fall-through possible on a
49	  fresh node before any key has been installed.
50	
51	//axhos_step
52	. *Ensure the admin user has a known password*: {burp_privileged_user}
53	  must be a member of the Administrators group and must have a password
54	  the operator knows. This credential is required for caparison's
55	  phase-1 password fallback — the operator types it once on `/dev/tty`;
56	  caparison itself never handles the password. Verify or set with
57	  `lusrmgr.msc` (GUI) or from an elevated cmd prompt:
58	  `net user <username> <newpassword>`.
59	
60	//axhos_step
61	. *Apply security-sensitive registry edits*: each entry requires
62	  elevation. Use `reg add` from an elevated cmd prompt or
63	  `New-ItemProperty` from an elevated PowerShell prompt.
64	+
65	`DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion` = `0` (DWORD) under
66	`HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\PasswordLess\Device`:
67	+
68	----
69	reg add "HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\PasswordLess\Device" /v DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f
70	----
71	+
72	Required for the netplwiz auto-login GUI to expose the "Users must
73	enter a user name and password" checkbox on Windows 10/11. Without
74	this key the netplwiz step has no checkbox to uncheck.
75	+
76	`PlatformAoAcOverride` = `0` (DWORD) under
77	`HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power`:
78	+
79	----
80	reg add "HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power" /v PlatformAoAcOverride /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f
81	----
82	+
83	Required on Modern Standby (AlwaysOnAlwaysConnected) systems so
84	caparison's `powercfg` sleep/hibernate disable will actually take.
85	Without the override, `powercfg /change standby-timeout-ac 0` is
86	silently demoted to a no-op.
87	+
88	Both keys are asserted by {bust_invigilate_windows} as
89	operator-managed posture facts; mismatches there point back at this
90	handbook step.
91	
92	//axhos_step
93	. *Install Tailscale*: download the Tailscale for Windows MSI from
94	  https://tailscale.com/download and run the installer. The installer
95	  registers a Windows service named `Tailscale` and adds a system-tray
96	  icon. No configuration is needed at this stage.
97	
98	//axhos_step
99	. *Toggle Tailscale Run-Unattended*: open the Tailscale system-tray
100	  icon → Preferences → enable "Run unattended". This lets the Tailscale
101	  service start at boot and stay up without an interactive desktop
102	  session — required because the host runs without a console login
103	  during automated tests. Note: {bust_caparison_windows} sets the
104	  Tailscale service `StartupType` to `Automatic`, but the service can
105	  only deliver useful traffic once Run-Unattended is on.
106	
107	//axhos_step
108	. *Authenticate Tailscale to the operator's tailnet*: open the
109	  Tailscale system-tray icon → Sign in → complete the browser-based
110	  first-time authentication. This binds the host to the operator's
111	  tailnet so the BURN profile's tailnet hostname (or `100.x.y.z`
112	  address) becomes a stable transport address that persists across
113	  reboots.
114	
115	//axhos_step
116	. *Configure netplwiz auto-login*: press Windows + R, type `netplwiz`,
117	  press Enter. In the User Accounts dialog, select {burp_privileged_user}.
118	  Uncheck "Users must enter a user name and password to use this
119	  computer" (this checkbox is visible only after the
120	  `DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion` registry edit in the step above).
121	  Click Apply, then enter the admin password twice when prompted. The
122	  host now boots straight to a logged-in admin desktop, which is the
123	  substrate Tailscale Run-Unattended and the OpenSSH server depend on
124	  for unattended operation.
125	
126	//axhog_guarantee
127	No remote contact occurred and no local file was written by the
128	handbook itself. The operator has the procedure rendered; performing
129	the steps above establishes the operator-managed posture scope that
130	{bust_caparison_windows} and {bust_invigilate_windows} depend on.
131	
132	//axhoc_completion
133	Exit 0 after the handbook content is printed.
134	Non-zero only when the handbook content cannot be located or rendered.
135

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_bash}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell,
5	for Linux and macOS nodes.
6	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
7	{bujb_workload_user} via the letter-agnostic
8	`zbujb_obliterate_workload` helper in {bujb_jurisdiction} — the
9	single greppable destroy verb. On Linux the helper runs `userdel -r`
10	and purges `/home/<user>`; on macOS it runs `sysadminctl -deleteUser`
11	and purges `/Users/<user>`. Each step is idempotent on absent state.
12	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
13	admin authorized_keys.
14	
15	//axhopt_typed_parameter
16	{burp_investiture}
17	
18	//axhoq_precondition
19	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
20	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
21	
22	//axhoq_precondition
23	sshd is reachable on the node and {burp_privileged_user} authenticates
24	by key alone using {burp_privileged_key_file}. On Linux/Mac admin
25	trust is established via {busn_caparison}, which wraps the
26	operator-managed `ssh-copy-id` flow rather than performing autonomous
27	SSH-trust hardening (only Windows OpenSSH has the autonomous
28	phase-1/phase-2 ceremony).
29	
30	//axhoq_precondition
31	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
32	station.
33	
34	//axhos_step
35	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
36	{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
37	missing or stale admin trust.
38	
39	//axhos_step
40	. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`. On Linux
41	the helper runs `sudo -n userdel -r` and `sudo -n rm -rf /home/<user>`;
42	on macOS it runs `sudo -n sysadminctl -deleteUser` and
43	`sudo -n rm -rf /Users/<user>`. Idempotent on absent state.
44	
45	//axhos_step
46	. *Create workload*: provision {bujb_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
47	ssh-only account.
48	
49	//axhos_step
50	. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
51	(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
52	the shell-letter `b` `command=` directive below — locked spec content,
53	mirrored at `BUJB_command_b` in {bujb_jurisdiction}:
54	+
55	[listing]
56	----
57	command="/bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
58	----
59	
60	//axhos_step
61	. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
62	account at the shell-letter `b` destination path hardcoded in
63	{bujb_jurisdiction}.
64	
65	//axhos_step
66	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
67	exit 0.
68	
69	//axhog_guarantee
70	On success the node has exactly one workload account named
71	{bujb_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
72	outbound (to GitHub or equivalent) key trust in place.
73	
74	//axhoc_completion
75	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
76	Non-zero on admin SSH failure, account-management failure, key-write
77	failure, or workload validation failure.

content.content:
1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_wsl}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
5	for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
6	(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}). On Windows the workload identity
7	inhabits *three namespaces*: a Windows local user (created via
8	`net.exe user /add` plus profile registration in
9	`HKLM\...\ProfileList`) provides the SSH authentication boundary that
10	Windows OpenSSH discovers; the workload user's *own* registered WSL
11	distribution (populated in their `HKCU\Lxss` via `wsl --import` from
12	a seed tarball) provides the distribution registration the workload's
13	session resolves; and a Linux user inside that workload-owned
14	distribution (created via `useradd`) provides the WSL execution
15	context entered by the locked-down `command=` directive. The third
16	namespace is forced by Microsoft's per-user WSL design: distributions
17	register only in the running user's `HKCU\Lxss`, and admin's
18	distributions are invisible to workload sessions.
19	
20	The mechanism is *SSH-as-workload conducted by the privileged
21	orchestrator*. The orchestrator never runs `wsl` as the workload
22	user directly. Instead it places a *bare* workload authorized_keys
23	entry (no `command=`), opens a workload SSH session through that
24	entry — which constitutes a real Windows logon for
25	{bujb_workload_user}, with HKCU mounted — and from inside that
26	session runs `wsl --import` (writing to the workload's HKCU), the
27	inner-WSL `useradd` + `passwd --lock`, and the workload privkey
28	placement. The orchestrator then replaces the bare entry with the
29	locked-down `command=` form and validates the round-trip through
30	that locked form.
31	
32	The Windows workload user is the same shape minted by the c branch
33	({bust_garrison_cygwin}); the workload-owned WSL distribution and
34	its inner Linux user are layered on top so the SSH session lands as
35	the named WSL user (via the locked-down `command=` directive's
36	`wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distribution's default user.
37	
38	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload state across all
39	three namespaces via `zbujb_obliterate_workload` in
40	{bujb_jurisdiction} — the single greppable destroy verb. The
41	covenant scope spans the Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\`
42	Windows profile directory (by convention containing the workload's
43	WSL filesystem root, so purging the profile transitively destroys
44	the workload's WSL distribution registration's on-disk filesystem
45	and the HKCU hive that referenced it), the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`
46	Cygwin home, and the WSL-side Linux user inside admin's `rbtww-main`
47	(vestige from prior garrison shapes; idempotent on absent state).
48	
49	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
50	admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
51	{busn_caparison} on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening
52	inside the WSL distribution remains operator-manual.
53	
54	//axhopt_typed_parameter
55	{burp_investiture}
56	
57	//axhoq_precondition
58	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
59	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
60	
61	//axhoq_precondition
62	{busn_caparison} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
63	{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
64	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
65	entry point); the workload SSH session is then opened from that
66	foothold using the workload pubkey just placed by garrison.
67	
68	//axhoq_precondition
69	{bust_invigilate_windows} passes for this {burp_investiture}: admin
70	SSH key trust is in place, the operator-managed registry posture is
71	correct, the Tailscale service is registered and set to Automatic,
72	powercfg sleep/hibernate are disabled, and the `rbtww-main` WSL
73	distribution (canonical name hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution` in
74	{bujb_jurisdiction}) is registered under the admin user. Garrison
75	runs invigilate as its first action so a missing or stale admin
76	posture surfaces with a fact-named diagnostic rather than as an
77	opaque downstream failure.
78	
79	//axhoq_precondition
80	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
81	station.
82	
83	//axhos_step
84	. *Assert admin posture*: invoke `{bust_invigilate_windows}` for this
85	{burp_investiture}. Failure surfaces a fact-named diagnostic
86	({bust_handbook_windows} pointer for operator-managed facts;
87	{bust_caparison_windows} pointer for caparison-managed facts) and
88	aborts garrison before any destructive workload work.
89	
90	//axhos_step
91	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
92	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
93	only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.
94	
95	//axhos_step
96	. *Export seed*: export admin's `rbtww-main` distribution to a
97	workload-readable seed tarball under a path inside the workload's
98	profile (admin can write there before the workload first logs in,
99	and the workload's session can read it). Caparison has staged
100	`rbtww-main` and invigilate has verified its presence as a
101	precondition; this step takes that presence as given. The tarball
102	is the sole on-disk artifact carrying admin's `rbtww-main` contents
103	into the workload's identity scope; garrison removes it on success.
104	
105	//axhos_step
106	. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`, the
107	letter-agnostic destroy helper. On Windows the helper executes a
108	single PowerShell session over admin SSH that purges the Windows
109	SAM entry (`net.exe user /delete`), the Windows profile directory
110	(`C:\Users\<user>\`, including any prior workload-owned WSL
111	filesystem under that path), the Cygwin home
112	(`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`), and the WSL-side Linux user inside
113	admin's `rbtww-main` (vestige from prior garrison shapes;
114	idempotent on absent state).
115	
116	//axhos_step
117	. *Create Windows workload account*: provision {bujb_workload_user}
118	as a Windows local user via
119	`net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes` (matching the c
120	branch), then register the resulting SID under
121	`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList` so
122	Windows OpenSSH builds a complete logon session for the workload
123	on inbound SSH (Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383). No Linux user is
124	created inside admin's rbtww-main; the workload's Linux user is
125	minted inside the workload's *own* imported distribution in the
126	SSH-as-workload step below.
127	
128	//axhos_step
129	. *Place bare workload trust*: write the workload pubkey (derived
130	via `ssh-keygen -y` from {burp_workload_key_file}) to the
131	Windows-side authorized_keys at
132	`/mnt/c/Users/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/authorized_keys` so Windows
133	OpenSSH discovers it natively. The directory is created with
134	`mkdir -p` and chmod 700; the file is written and chmod 600. No
135	POSIX `chown` is performed — `/mnt/c/...` ownership is meaningless
136	to Windows OpenSSH, which inspects NTFS ACLs; the inherited NTFS
137	ACL from the parent profile directory is admin-owned and read-only
138	for non-admins, satisfying StrictModes. The entry is *bare* — no
139	`command=` directive — because the locked-down `command=` would
140	route through a WSL distribution that has not yet been imported
141	under the workload's HKCU. The bare-then-lockdown pattern is
142	structural to this ceremony; the workload pubkey is derived from
143	{burp_workload_key_file} and owned by the operator, so the brief
144	window in which the bare entry is in place exposes only the
145	operator's own running garrison.
146	
147	//axhos_step
148	. *Initialize workload's WSL via SSH-as-workload*: open an SSH
149	session as {bujb_workload_user} using {burp_workload_key_file}
150	through the just-placed bare authorized_keys entry. This session
151	constitutes a real Windows logon for the workload, with HKCU
152	mounted. Inside that session, perform in order: `wsl.exe --import
153	rbtww-main <workload-wsl-root> <seed-tarball-path>` to register
154	the workload's own rbtww-main in `HKCU\Lxss`, where
155	`<workload-wsl-root>` is a workload-writable directory inside the
156	workload's Windows profile by convention;
157	`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root useradd
158	--create-home --shell /bin/bash ${BUJB_workload_user}` to mint
159	the inner Linux workload user; `passwd --lock` (root inside the
160	distribution) on that Linux user to enforce SSH-key-only on the
161	inner account; and plant {burp_workload_key_file}'s contents at
162	`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519` (chmod 600) inside
163	the workload's distribution so the Linux session can use it for
164	outbound (e.g., GitHub). On exit, remove the seed tarball from
165	the admin side — the seed has served its purpose.
166	
167	//axhos_step
168	. *Lock down workload trust*: rewrite the workload's authorized_keys
169	file with the locked-down `command=` form, replacing the bare entry
170	placed earlier. The rewrite happens from a workload SSH session
171	because the file is workload-owned (per the icacls grants in step 5)
172	and the workload's session has direct write privilege without
173	ownership gymnastics. The directive content is locked spec text,
174	mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in {bujb_jurisdiction};
175	`${BUJB_workload_user}` is substituted with the canonical workload
176	user at directive-emit time:
177	+
178	[listing]
179	----
180	command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
181	----
182	
183	//axhos_step
184	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command,
185	verify exit 0. Validates the *locked-down* form: the workload
186	session is routed through `command=`, which dispatches to the
187	workload's own imported `rbtww-main` distribution and runs the
188	inner Linux user's shell.
189	
190	//axhog_guarantee
191	On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
192	{bujb_workload_user} mirrored across all three namespaces: the
193	Windows local user (auth boundary, with profile registered in
194	`HKLM\...\ProfileList`), the workload-owned WSL distribution
195	`rbtww-main` (registered in workload's `HKCU\Lxss`), and the
196	WSL Linux user inside that distribution (execution context).
197	
198	//axhoc_completion
199	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
200	Non-zero on invigilate-windows assertion failure, admin SSH failure,
201	seed tarball export failure, account-management failure in any
202	namespace, `wsl --import` failure, key-write failure, or workload
203	validation failure.
204

three namespaces via `zbujb_obliterate_workload` in
{bujb_jurisdiction} — the single greppable destroy verb. The
covenant scope spans the Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\`
Windows profile directory (by convention containing the workload's
WSL filesystem root, so purging the profile transitively destroys
the workload's WSL distribution registration's on-disk filesystem
and the HKCU hive that referenced it), the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`
Cygwin home, and the WSL-side Linux user inside admin's `rbtww-main`
(vestige from prior garrison shapes; idempotent on absent state).

Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_caparison} on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening
inside the WSL distribution remains operator-manual.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
{busn_caparison} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
entry point); the workload SSH session is then opened from that
foothold using the workload pubkey just placed by garrison.

//axhoq_precondition
{bust_invigilate_windows} passes for this {burp_investiture}: admin
SSH key trust is in place, the operator-managed registry posture is
correct, the Tailscale service is registered and set to Automatic,
powercfg sleep/hibernate are disabled, and the `rbtww-main` WSL
distribution (canonical name hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution` in
{bujb_jurisdiction}) is registered under the admin user. Garrison
runs invigilate as its first action so a missing or stale admin
posture surfaces with a fact-named diagnostic rather than as an
opaque downstream failure.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Assert admin posture*: invoke `{bust_invigilate_windows}` for this
{burp_investiture}. Failure surfaces a fact-named diagnostic
({bust_handbook_windows} pointer for operator-managed facts;
{bust_caparison_windows} pointer for caparison-managed facts) and
aborts garrison before any destructive workload work.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Export seed*: export admin's `rbtww-main` distribution to a
workload-readable seed tarball under a path inside the workload's
profile (admin can write there before the workload first logs in,
and the workload's session can read it). Caparison has staged
`rbtww-main` and invigilate has verified its presence as a
precondition; this step takes that presence as given. The tarball
is the sole on-disk artifact carrying admin's `rbtww-main` contents
into the workload's identity scope; garrison removes it on success.

//axhos_step
. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`, the
letter-agnostic destroy helper. On Windows the helper executes a
single PowerShell session over admin SSH that purges the Windows
SAM entry (`net.exe user /delete`), the Windows profile directory
(`C:\Users\<user>\`, including any prior workload-owned WSL
filesystem under that path), the Cygwin home
(`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`), and the WSL-side Linux user inside
admin's `rbtww-main` (vestige from prior garrison shapes;
idempotent on absent state).

//axhos_step
. *Create Windows workload account*: provision {bujb_workload_user}
as a Windows local user via
`net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes` (matching the c
branch), then register the resulting SID under
`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList` so
Windows OpenSSH builds a complete logon session for the workload
on inbound SSH (Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383). No Linux user is
created inside admin's rbtww-main; the workload's Linux user is
minted inside the workload's *own* imported distribution in the
SSH-as-workload step below.

//axhos_step
. *Place bare workload trust*: write the workload pubkey (derived
via `ssh-keygen -y` from {burp_workload_key_file}) to the
Windows-side authorized_keys at
`/mnt/c/Users/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/authorized_keys` so Windows
OpenSSH discovers it natively. The directory is created with
`mkdir -p` and chmod 700; the file is written and chmod 600. No
POSIX `chown` is performed — `/mnt/c/...` ownership is meaningless
to Windows OpenSSH, which inspects NTFS ACLs; the inherited NTFS
ACL from the parent profile directory is admin-owned and read-only
for non-admins, satisfying StrictModes. The entry is *bare* — no
`command=` directive — because the locked-down `command=` would
route through a WSL distribution that has not yet been imported
under the workload's HKCU. The bare-then-lockdown pattern is
structural to this ceremony; the workload pubkey is derived from
{burp_workload_key_file} and owned by the operator, so the brief
window in which the bare entry is in place exposes only the
operator's own running garrison.

//axhos_step
. *Initialize workload's WSL via SSH-as-workload*: open an SSH
session as {bujb_workload_user} using {burp_workload_key_file}
through the just-placed bare authorized_keys entry. This session
constitutes a real Windows logon for the workload, with HKCU
mounted. Inside that session, perform in order: `wsl.exe --import
rbtww-main <workload-wsl-root> <seed-tarball-path>` to register
the workload's own rbtww-main in `HKCU\Lxss`, where
`<workload-wsl-root>` is a workload-writable directory inside the
workload's Windows profile by convention;
`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root useradd
--create-home --shell /bin/bash ${BUJB_workload_user}` to mint
the inner Linux workload user; `passwd --lock` (root inside the
distribution) on that Linux user to enforce SSH-key-only on the
inner account; and plant {burp_workload_key_file}'s contents at
`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519` (chmod 600) inside
the workload's distribution so the Linux session can use it for
outbound (e.g., GitHub). On exit, remove the seed tarball from
the admin side — the seed has served its purpose.

//axhos_step
. *Lock down workload trust*: rewrite the workload's authorized_keys
file with the locked-down `command=` form, replacing the bare entry
placed earlier. The rewrite happens from a workload SSH session
because the file is workload-owned (per the icacls grants in step 5)
and the workload's session has direct write privilege without
ownership gymnastics. The directive content is locked spec text,
mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in {bujb_jurisdiction};
`${BUJB_workload_user}` is substituted with the canonical workload
user at directive-emit time:
+
[listing]
----
command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command,
verify exit 0. Validates the *locked-down* form: the workload
session is routed through `command=`, which dispatches to the
workload's own imported `rbtww-main` distribution and runs the
inner Linux user's shell.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
{bujb_workload_user} mirrored across all three namespaces: the
Windows local user (auth boundary, with profile registered in
`HKLM\...\ProfileList`), the workload-owned WSL distribution
`rbtww-main` (registered in workload's `HKCU\Lxss`), and the
WSL Linux user inside that distribution (execution context).

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on invigilate-windows assertion failure, admin SSH failure,
seed tarball export failure, account-management failure in any
namespace, `wsl --import` failure, key-write failure, or workload
validation failure.

//axhob_operation
{bust_caparison_macos}

NOTE: {busn_caparison} ceremony on macOS — single-phase admin host
posture establishment over an already-established key-auth admin SSH
session. Operator-managed scope per {bust_handbook_macos} covers the
one-time SSH-trust setup: enable Remote Login on the host, then run
`ssh-copy-id` from the operator's station to place the admin pubkey.
Caparison wraps that operator-manual flow rather than performing
autonomous SSH-trust hardening — only Windows OpenSSH has the
autonomous phase-1/phase-2 ceremony. Once admin key trust is in place,
caparison reinforces Remote Login (idempotent), applies `pmset` power
configuration, and sets the Tailscale system daemon to load at boot.
Nuclear: every invocation purges and re-establishes the
caparison-managed posture from scratch — no skip-if-present logic.
Closing step runs {bust_invigilate_macos} as the post-completion
check.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile targets a macOS node.

//axhoq_precondition
{bust_handbook_macos} operator-manual scope is complete: Remote Login
has been enabled at least once so the host accepts SSH, `ssh-copy-id`
has been run by the operator from their station to place the admin
pubkey, Tailscale (standalone download) has been installed and
authenticated to the operator's tailnet, and a sudoers entry grants
NOPASSWD execution to {burp_privileged_user} for the
caparison-managed commands. Consult {bust_handbook_macos} for the
full list; caparison presupposes this scope, neither writes nor
repairs it.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_privileged_user} is a member of the admin group and authenticates
by key alone via {burp_privileged_key_file}.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_privileged_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only (no password fallback).
Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust — the diagnostic
points to {bust_handbook_macos} since admin trust is operator-managed
(ssh-copy-id).

//axhos_step
. *Enable Remote Login*: invoke `sudo -n systemsetup -setremotelogin
on`. Idempotent on already-enabled state. Operator established this
once via System Settings so ssh-copy-id could reach the host;
caparison reinforces the setting so a manual toggle-off does not
silently drop the node.

//axhos_step
. *Apply power configuration*: invoke `sudo -n pmset -a sleep 0`,
`sudo -n pmset -a disablesleep 1`, and `sudo -n pmset -a hibernatemode
0`. Idempotent. The settings span all power sources so battery hosts
behave identically to wall-powered hosts.

//axhos_step
. *Set Tailscale daemon to auto-load*: invoke `sudo -n launchctl
enable system/com.tailscale.tailscaled` followed by `sudo -n launchctl
kickstart -k system/com.tailscale.tailscaled`. Idempotent. The
Tailscale standalone installer registers the daemon as a system
LaunchDaemon; caparison only ensures it loads at boot and is currently
running. Tailscale itself was installed and authenticated by the
operator per {bust_handbook_macos}; caparison only sets the daemon
shape.

//axhos_step
. *Post-completion check — run invigilate-macos*: invoke
{bust_invigilate_macos} for this {burp_investiture}. Non-zero exit
indicates a caparison operation did not take or an operator-managed
posture fact is missing; the diagnostic from {bust_invigilate_macos}
names the failing fact and points the operator at the right repair
surface.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has admin SSH reachable under key-only auth as
{burp_privileged_user}, Remote Login enabled, pmset sleep and
hibernate disabled across all power sources, the Tailscale system
daemon loaded and running with auto-start at boot, and
{bust_invigilate_macos} has confirmed every posture fact end-to-end.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the post-completion {bust_invigilate_macos} passes.
Non-zero on admin SSH failure, `systemsetup` failure, any `pmset`
failure, Tailscale `launchctl enable` or `kickstart` failure, or
post-completion invigilate failure.

//axhob_operation
{bust_caparison_linux}

NOTE: {busn_caparison} ceremony on Linux — single-phase admin host
posture establishment over an already-established key-auth admin SSH
session. Operator-managed scope per {bust_handbook_linux} covers the
one-time SSH-trust setup: install and enable OpenSSH server, then run
`ssh-copy-id` from the operator's station to place the admin pubkey.
Caparison wraps that operator-manual flow rather than performing
autonomous SSH-trust hardening — only Windows OpenSSH has the
autonomous phase-1/phase-2 ceremony. Once admin key trust is in
place, caparison reinforces the sshd service (idempotent), masks
systemd sleep targets on laptop/desktop hosts, and enables the
`tailscaled` service. Nuclear: every invocation purges and
re-establishes the caparison-managed posture from scratch — no
skip-if-present logic. Closing step runs {bust_invigilate_linux} as
the post-completion check.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile targets a Linux node running systemd.

//axhoq_precondition
{bust_handbook_linux} operator-manual scope is complete: OpenSSH
server is installed and reachable, `ssh-copy-id` has been run by the
operator from their station to place the admin pubkey, Tailscale has
been installed and authenticated to the operator's tailnet, and a
sudoers entry grants NOPASSWD execution to {burp_privileged_user} for
the caparison-managed commands. Consult {bust_handbook_linux} for the
full list; caparison presupposes this scope, neither writes nor
repairs it.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_privileged_user} is a member of the sudo (or wheel) group and
authenticates by key alone via {burp_privileged_key_file}.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_privileged_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only (no password fallback).
Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust — the diagnostic
points to {bust_handbook_linux} since admin trust is operator-managed
(ssh-copy-id).

//axhos_step
. *Reinforce sshd service*: invoke `sudo -n systemctl enable --now
sshd`. Idempotent. Operator established sshd reachability once per
{bust_handbook_linux}; caparison reinforces the enabled+active shape
so a manual `systemctl disable` does not silently drop the node on
next boot.

//axhos_step
. *Mask sleep targets on laptop/desktop hosts*: when the BURN profile
identifies the host as a laptop or desktop class (servers skip this
step — they typically do not advertise sleep targets), invoke
`sudo -n systemctl mask sleep.target suspend.target hibernate.target
hybrid-sleep.target`. Idempotent on already-masked state. Masking
prevents systemd from honouring sleep requests from upower or login
managers; the host stays reachable across lid-close events and idle
timers.

//axhos_step
. *Enable tailscaled service*: invoke `sudo -n systemctl enable --now
tailscaled`. Idempotent. The Tailscale install drops the systemd unit;
caparison only ensures it is enabled at boot and currently active.
Tailscale itself was installed and authenticated by the operator per
{bust_handbook_linux}; caparison only sets the service shape.

//axhos_step
. *Post-completion check — run invigilate-linux*: invoke
{bust_invigilate_linux} for this {burp_investiture}. Non-zero exit
indicates a caparison operation did not take or an operator-managed
posture fact is missing; the diagnostic from {bust_invigilate_linux}
names the failing fact and points the operator at the right repair
surface.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has admin SSH reachable under key-only auth as
{burp_privileged_user}, sshd enabled and active, sleep targets masked
where the BURN class warrants it, the `tailscaled` service enabled
and active, and {bust_invigilate_linux} has confirmed every posture
fact end-to-end.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the post-completion {bust_invigilate_linux} passes.
Non-zero on admin SSH failure, sshd `systemctl` failure, sleep-target
mask failure, tailscaled `systemctl` failure, or post-completion
invigilate failure.

+`sudo -n systemctl mask sleep.target suspend.target hibernate.target
+hybrid-sleep.target`. Idempotent on already-masked state. Masking
+prevents systemd from honouring sleep requests from upower or login
+managers; the host stays reachable across lid-close events and idle
+timers.
+//axhos_step
+. *Enable tailscaled service*: invoke `sudo -n systemctl enable --now
+tailscaled`. Idempotent. The Tailscale install drops the systemd unit;
+caparison only ensures it is enabled at boot and currently active.
+Tailscale itself was installed and authenticated by the operator per
+{bust_handbook_linux}; caparison only sets the service shape.
+//axhos_step
+. *Post-completion check — run invigilate-linux*: invoke
+{bust_invigilate_linux} for this {burp_investiture}. Non-zero exit
+indicates a caparison operation did not take or an operator-managed
+posture fact is missing; the diagnostic from {bust_invigilate_linux}
+names the failing fact and points the operator at the right repair
+surface.
+//axhog_guarantee
+On success the node has admin SSH reachable under key-only auth as
+{burp_privileged_user}, sshd enabled and active, sleep targets masked
+where the BURN class warrants it, the `tailscaled` service enabled
+and active, and {bust_invigilate_linux} has confirmed every posture
+fact end-to-end.
+//axhoc_completion
+Exit 0 when the post-completion {bust_invigilate_linux} passes.
+Non-zero on admin SSH failure, sshd `systemctl` failure, sleep-target
+mask failure, tailscaled `systemctl` failure, or post-completion
+invigilate failure.

{bust_invigilate_macos}

NOTE: {busn_invigilate} ceremony on macOS — read-only host posture
verification. Asserts each posture fact (admin SSH key trust, Remote
Login state, `pmset` sleep and hibernate disabled, Tailscale system
daemon registered and running) matches its expected value. No
mutations, no remediation. Failure pointers discriminate
operator-managed posture (→ {bust_handbook_macos}) from
caparison-managed posture (→ {bust_caparison_macos}).

Invocation contexts: {bust_caparison_macos} runs invigilate-macos as
its post-completion check (a non-zero exit signals that a caparison
operation did not take or that the operator-managed posture is not in
place); {bust_garrison_bash} runs it as its precondition assertion
before any destructive workload work on macOS nodes, so a missing or
stale admin posture surfaces with fact-named guidance rather than an
opaque downstream failure.

Read commands run over the admin SSH session opened with
{burp_privileged_key_file}: `systemsetup -getremotelogin` for Remote
Login state, `pmset -g custom` for current sleep settings, `launchctl
print system/com.tailscale.tailscaled` for the Tailscale daemon. Bash
-side BCG-compliant matching extracts each fact and compares to its
expected value.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile targets a macOS node reachable as
{burp_privileged_user} under key-only authentication using
{burp_privileged_key_file}. Invigilate does not establish that trust —
it presupposes it; admin SSH failure surfaces as the first posture
fact.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only (no password fallback).
Failure here is the first posture fact and surfaces a missing or
stale admin trust — the diagnostic points to {bust_handbook_macos}
since admin trust on macOS is operator-managed (ssh-copy-id).

//axhos_step
. *Assert Remote Login enabled*: invoke `systemsetup -getremotelogin`
and assert the output reports `Remote Login: On`. Mismatch points to
{bust_caparison_macos} — caparison reinforces Remote Login on every
invocation, so a disabled state indicates caparison either did not
run or its `systemsetup` operation did not take.

//axhos_step
. *Assert pmset sleep disabled*: invoke `pmset -g custom` and assert
the `sleep`, `disablesleep`, and `hibernatemode` values match
caparison's settings (`0`, `1`, `0` respectively) on every advertised
power source. Mismatch points to {bust_caparison_macos} — these are
the values caparison's `pmset -a` calls set; drift indicates
caparison either did not run or a power-management GUI undid the
setting.

//axhos_step
. *Assert Tailscale daemon registered*: invoke `launchctl print
system/com.tailscale.tailscaled` and assert the daemon record exists.
Absence indicates Tailscale was never installed (the standalone
installer registers this system LaunchDaemon); the diagnostic points
to {bust_handbook_macos}.

//axhos_step
. *Assert Tailscale daemon running*: from the same `launchctl print`
output, extract the `state` field and assert it equals `running`.
A non-running state points to {bust_caparison_macos} — caparison
issues `launchctl kickstart` after `enable`, so a stopped daemon
indicates caparison either did not run or the kickstart failed.

//axhog_guarantee
On success every posture fact has been asserted to match its expected
value, the running admin SSH session was used only for read commands,
and no remote or local mutation occurred. The node is in the shape
{bust_caparison_macos} produces and {bust_garrison_bash} requires on
macOS.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when every posture fact matches its expected value.
Non-zero on the first mismatch, with a diagnostic naming the failing
fact, its expected value, its observed value, and the pointer
({bust_handbook_macos} for operator-managed facts;
{bust_caparison_macos} for caparison-managed facts).

//axhob_operation
{bust_caparison_macos}

NOTE: {busn_caparison} ceremony on macOS — single-phase admin host
posture establishment over an already-established key-auth admin SSH
session. Operator-managed scope per {bust_handbook_macos} covers the
one-time SSH-trust setup: enable Remote Login on the host, then run
`ssh-copy-id` from the operator's station to place the admin pubkey.
Caparison wraps that operator-manual flow rather than performing
autonomous SSH-trust hardening — only Windows OpenSSH has the
autonomous phase-1/phase-2 ceremony. Once admin key trust is in place,
caparison reinforces Remote Login (idempotent), applies `pmset` power
configuration, and sets the Tailscale system daemon to load at boot.
Nuclear: every invocation purges and re-establishes the
caparison-managed posture from scratch — no skip-if-present logic.
Closing step runs {bust_invigilate_macos} as the post-completion
check.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile targets a macOS node.

//axhoq_precondition
{bust_handbook_macos} operator-manual scope is complete: Remote Login
has been enabled at least once so the host accepts SSH, `ssh-copy-id`
has been run by the operator from their station to place the admin
pubkey, Tailscale (standalone download) has been installed and
authenticated to the operator's tailnet, and a sudoers entry grants
NOPASSWD execution to {burp_privileged_user} for the
caparison-managed commands. Consult {bust_handbook_macos} for the
full list; caparison presupposes this scope, neither writes nor
repairs it.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_privileged_user} is a member of the admin group and authenticates
by key alone via {burp_privileged_key_file}.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_privileged_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only (no password fallback).
Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust — the diagnostic
points to {bust_handbook_macos} since admin trust is operator-managed
(ssh-copy-id).

//axhos_step
. *Enable Remote Login*: invoke `sudo -n systemsetup -setremotelogin
on`. Idempotent on already-enabled state. Operator established this
once via System Settings so ssh-copy-id could reach the host;
caparison reinforces the setting so a manual toggle-off does not
silently drop the node.

//axhos_step
. *Apply power configuration*: invoke `sudo -n pmset -a sleep 0`,
`sudo -n pmset -a disablesleep 1`, and `sudo -n pmset -a hibernatemode
0`. Idempotent. The settings span all power sources so battery hosts
behave identically to wall-powered hosts.

//axhos_step
. *Set Tailscale daemon to auto-load*: invoke `sudo -n launchctl
enable system/com.tailscale.tailscaled` followed by `sudo -n launchctl
kickstart -k system/com.tailscale.tailscaled`. Idempotent. The
Tailscale standalone installer registers the daemon as a system
LaunchDaemon; caparison only ensures it loads at boot and is currently
running. Tailscale itself was installed and authenticated by the
operator per {bust_handbook_macos}; caparison only sets the daemon
shape.

//axhos_step
. *Post-completion check — run invigilate-macos*: invoke
{bust_invigilate_macos} for this {burp_investiture}. Non-zero exit
indicates a caparison operation did not take or an operator-managed
posture fact is missing; the diagnostic from {bust_invigilate_macos}
names the failing fact and points the operator at the right repair
surface.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has admin SSH reachable under key-only auth as
{burp_privileged_user}, Remote Login enabled, pmset sleep and
hibernate disabled across all power sources, the Tailscale system
daemon loaded and running with auto-start at boot, and
{bust_invigilate_macos} has confirmed every posture fact end-to-end.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the post-completion {bust_invigilate_macos} passes.
Non-zero on admin SSH failure, `systemsetup` failure, any `pmset`
failure, Tailscale `launchctl enable` or `kickstart` failure, or
post-completion invigilate failure.

stale admin posture surfaces with fact-named guidance rather than an
opaque downstream failure.

print system/com.tailscale.tailscaled` for the Tailscale daemon. Bash

expected value.

{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition

{burp_privileged_user} under key-only authentication using

since admin trust on macOS is operator-managed (ssh-copy-id).

On success every posture fact has been asserted to match its expected
